_meta.method: Compiled from (1) Sōtōshū's official 'temples welcoming foreign practitioners' directory at sotozen-navi.com (page 1 of 12), (2) Rinzai-Ōbaku Federation honzan list with English Wikipedia infobox cross-checks, (3) widely-attested major Sōtō / Rinzai senmon-sōdō (training monasteries).
_meta.coverageEstimate: Honzan saturation for Rinzai, plus a representative slice of foreign-friendly parish temples and training monasteries; Sōtōshū's full directory has 228 foreign-friendly entries, only first page included this round.
knownGaps: Sōtōshū foreign-friendly directory pages 2-12 (~209 more parish temples)
Many Rinzai sub-honzan and bekkaku jiin
Most Sōtō senmon-sōdō (only the most-visible internationally are included)

places.name: Engaku-ji
places.notes: Founded 1282 by Mugaku Sogen; head temple of the Engaku-ji branch of Rinzai. Open daily for tourist visits and hosts public zazenkai.

places.name: Kenchō-ji
places.notes: Founded 1253 by Lanxi Daolong; oldest formal Zen training monastery in Japan; head temple of the Kenchō-ji branch of Rinzai.

places.name: Hōkō-ji
places.notes: Founded 1384; head temple of the Hōkō-ji branch of Rinzai.

places.name: Buttsū-ji
places.notes: Founded 1397 by Guchū Shūkyū; head temple of the Buttsū-ji branch of Rinzai.

places.name: Hōfuku-ji
places.notes: Founded 1242; head temple of the Hōfuku-ji branch of Rinzai. Sesshū studied here in his youth.

places.name: Kōgaku-ji
places.notes: Founded 1380 by Bassui Tokushō; head temple of the Kōgaku-ji branch of Rinzai.

places.name: Eigen-ji
places.notes: Founded 1361 by Jakushitsu Genkō; head temple of the Eigen-ji branch of Rinzai. Famous for autumn maples.

places.name: Sōji-ji Soin
places.notes: Original Sōji-ji on the Noto Peninsula; founded 1321 by Keizan Jōkin. After 1898 fire, head-temple status moved to Tsurumi (Yokohama); the Noto site retains 'Soin' (founding sub-temple) status.

places.name: Hosshin-ji
places.notes: Founded 1899; Sōtō Zen training monastery known internationally for foreign-practitioner programs led by Harada Daiun Sogaku, his successors and Hōzumi Gensho Roshi.

places.name: Zuiō-ji
places.notes: Sōtō Zen senmon-sōdō; refounded 1875. Several Western Sōtō teachers (Tangen Harada, Loori, others) trained here.

places.name: Daiyū-zan Saijō-ji
places.notes: Founded 1394 by Ryōan Emyō; Sōtō Zen 'bekkaku honzan' — special-rank head temple — and major pilgrimage destination.

places.name: Bukkoku-ji
places.lineage: Sōtō (small training temple — Tangen Roshi line)
places.notes: Small intensive Sōtō training temple founded by Harada Tangen Roshi; long-standing destination for Western Zen practitioners.

places.name: Shōfuku-ji
places.notes: Rinzai senmon-sōdō led for many years by Yamada Mumon Rōshi; major training destination in Kobe.

places.name: Tōkō-ji
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of temples that welcome foreign zazen practitioners (外国人参禅).

places.name: Unsei-ji
places.notes: Gesshōzan Unsei-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Zenshō-ji Nakatsugawa
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Dairyū-ji Oga
places.notes: Kaizōzan Dairyū-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Toyokawa-ji Hiratsuka
places.notes: Hiratsukazan Toyokawa-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Anraku-ji Yamanashi
places.notes: Hōfuzan Anraku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Fusai-ji Murakami
places.notes: Taiyōzan Fusai-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Kentoku-ji Nakagawa
places.notes: Ryūtakuzan Kentoku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Jūrin-ji Fujinomiya
places.notes: Fujisan Jūrin-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Daiun-ji Sakuragawa
places.notes: Tomiyasan Daiun-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Chōsen-ji Nakagawa
places.notes: Shirakusan Chōsen-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.notes: Tentokuzan Jōjō-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Eirin-ji Ogi
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Shōkaku-ji Hannō
places.notes: Fukuōzan Shōkaku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Taihei-ji Nagoya
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Dairyū-ji Shinjuku
places.notes: Kiunzan Dairyū-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples (central Tokyo).

places.name: Zenfuku-ji Harima
places.notes: Ōsawa Banshōzan Zenfuku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.
